info.title: getting started
info.text: In this demo we'll use MusicVAE, a VAE (Variational Autoencoder) model by Google Magenta that has learned a continuous latent space of melodies. When we sample from it, we get a short two-bar melody that sounds like something a human might hum. We'll play it back through a Tone.js synth and display each note on screen, highlighting it as it plays.

info.title: libraries
info.text: We'll need four libraries, the nn library, Tone.js for audio scheduling and synthesis, TensorFlow.js for running the model, and the MusicVAE module from @magenta/music which exposes the music_vae global variable.

info.title: global vars
info.text: We'll create three other global variables, mvae holds the MusicVAE model instance, synth is the Tone.js polyphonic synthesizer (polyphonic so it can play multiple notes at the same time if needed), and score is the div where we'll display the note names visually.

info.title: setup
info.text: In setup (which runs on page load) we create the MusicVAE model pointing at a small two-bar melody checkpoint, and a Tone.PolySynth routed to the audio output. We also create a button wired to playMusicVAE and a score display div styled for large readable text.

info.title: lazy init and sample
info.text: When the button is clicked, playMusicVAE initializes the model on the first call if needed. Then mvae.sample(1, temp) generates one melody at the given temp (temperature). Like with SketchRNN, temperature controls randomness: 1.0 is the default and gives varied but coherent results. The model returns samples in a quantized format, notes expressed as step numbers rather than real time.

info.title: extract note names
info.text: Each note in samples[0].notes is a MIDI-format object with a pitch property. We map those to human-readable note names using nn.midiToNote(), producing an array like ['C4', 'E4', 'G4', ...].

info.title: adjustStart
info.text: MusicVAE can sometimes return melodies that don't start at step zero. The adjustStart function normalizes this by subtracting the offset of the first note's start step from all notes, so playback always begins immediately rather than with an initial silence.

info.title: mvaeToTime
info.text: The mvaeToTime function converts from quantized step numbers to real seconds. We use a tempo of 120 BPM: each beat is 0.5 seconds, and each step is a sixteenth note (so 0.125 seconds per step). We compute a start time relative to Tone.now() and a dur (duration) from the difference between the start and end steps.

info.title: score display
info.text: The updateScore function creates a span for each note name, gives it a unique id (like note0, note1...) and adds it to the score div. We call notes.forEach(updateScore) to build the full visual sequence before playback begins.

info.title: scheduling playback
info.text: Back in our playMusicVAE function we loop over every note and schedule two things: synth.triggerAttackRelease() queues the audio event at the precise start time in Tone.js's scheduler, and setTimeout(highlightNote, delay, id) queues the visual highlight at the same moment. We convert the Tone time to milliseconds for setTimeout by subtracting Tone.now() and multiplying by 1000.

info.title: highlightNote
info.text: The highlightNote function first resets all note spans to grey, then colors the one matching the given id blue. This creates a simple "scrolling score" effect where the active note is highlighted in sync with the audio, making it easy to follow along.

info.title: when to use
info.text: These days it's clear that we can train all sorts of neural networks to synthesize all sorts of data, from text to images to musical sequances. As amazing and impressive as this is, we should think carefully about when writing logic ourselves is preferred over using a machine learning model.

info.title: closing thoughts
info.text: For example, part of the joy in composing algorithmic music is defining the musical logic ourselves, for many algorithmic composers this logic is the art. MusicVAE is a fascinating model with some interesting use cases, but don't forget that you can write you own musical logic as well, check out this demo for example.
